posts.name: Jeremy Holmes
posts.cooked:
After a very good showing by the '85 La Tache last week I busted a couple of other '85’s.

  • 1985 Bouchard Père et Fils Beaune 1er Cru Beaune du Château - France, Burgundy, Côte de Beaune, Beaune 1er Cru (12/22/2015)
    Immediately engaging with a wonderful perfume of ripe berries, root vegetables and aniseed. It get’s some cured meat notes with air. It is full and velvety in the mouth. It has real vinous sweetness and goes savoury on the finish with a suggestion of coffee and menthol. Near peak drinking now but in no danger of falling apart.
  • 1985 Domaine Louis Trapet Chambertin - France, Burgundy, Côte de Nuits, Chambertin Grand Cru (12/22/2015)
    Regal, strong wine with elements of perfume and beauty. It smells of earth, meat, iron and dark cherries. With air you get some engaging florals. It has great depth and breadth and is impeccably balanced and proportioned. It is liquid velvet in the mouth and has such good drive through the palate and really fans out on the finish.

Posted from CellarTracker

posts.name: Jeremy Holmes

posts.name: Howard Cooper
posts.cooked: Great. That is Bouchard’s cheaper easy drinking cuvée from a time when they were supposed to be underperforming. Fabulous.

posts.name: Jeremy Holmes
posts.cooked: and Louis Trapet are ‘supposed to’ be making better wine now. I guess I’m lucky in that I tend to ignore ‘supposed to’ and buy things that I reckon will drink well once they have the requisite age on them.

posts.name: Dusty Gillson
posts.cooked: I’m always on the lookout for 85’s, being my birthyear, but the pricing hurts a bit. . . must be that a lot of people seem to like 85’s. One of the best burgs I’ve had (top 5 at least) was a Rugiens from 85. Diamond Creeks from 85 are also excellent. In Bdx, I’ve tried way more 83’s and 86’s than 85’s, so I can’t speak to them very well.

user_status.description: Waiting until it is socially acceptable to drink again.

posts.name: Stuart BeauneHead Niemtzow
posts.cooked:
it was in hot demand when it came out…(you probably don’t remember) and it was difficult to come by in Burgundy and in the US, as it was the first universally acclaimed vintage there (1983 had detractors, though, to me is a much more interesting vintage and more ageworthy). So…it was expensive then…and with high demand. I think when the vintage’s reputation was downwardly revised…people drank many of them, as they seemed very drinkable, if not great.

And…since it was Parker who raved about the vintage…and Parker had relatively short (and pretty ridiculous) drinking windows…many people doubtlessly followed his advice in all ways.

posts.name: Richard Frankel
posts.cooked: In the last couple of years I’ve had a few Trapet '85 Chambertins and Chapelle-Chambertins. They’ve been wonderful well-aged burgundies. Wish I could find more.

posts.name: Claus Jeppesen

Whats not to like?

posts.name: Walker S.
posts.cooked:
In Champagne, I just tried the '85 Heidsieck Cuvee Charlie and it was outstanding, fresh. The '85 Bollinger RD was slightly oxidized and well past, though not without its up-side (just close your eyes real hard and use your imagination).

G. Noellat VR Beaumont '85 is full of ripe fruit, stunning.
Arnoux VR Suchots is full of earth, mushrooms, soy, very good but not really my style.
Leroy Gevrey Chamb is classic, lacy, well balanced acids, all the good stuff right now. My favorite of the '85’s I’ve had recently.

I haven’t had great experience with white Burgundy from '85 but haven’t had many. Would love to hear if others have had any recently.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
The 1985 Bonneau du Martray Corton Charlemagne is excellent.

The first 1985 red Burgundy I ever tasted was a 1985 Clos de la Roche Jeremy opened on my first visit to Domaine Dujac—and to Burgundy!

The 1985 Bordeaux are really lovely. Arguably the best Léoville Barton and -Las Cases of the decade for example. Reports of their decline are premature.

posts.name: Scott Brunson
posts.cooked:
John Morris:

But … but … 1985s were supposed to be long past their prime…

We were married in '85 and I am certainly past my prime.
I still like the wines though–backfilling as much as possible.

posts.name: John Gilman
posts.cooked:
The '85 Burgs were rightly praised out of the blocks and so many were drunk young, not because of their short projected windows of drinkability in the WA, but because they were so flat out delicious in their youth, with such stunningly pure and seductive fruit tones that it was almost impossible to resist opening them in that first five to ten year span. It is a fairly low acid vintage, so the wines were velvety from release, but they still had ripe tannins that continue to carry them nicely more than thirty years out from the vintage. The reason that their reputation dipped a bit around the turn of the century had to do with the phase they went into, with much of their early plumpness of fruit seemingly gone, the wines collectively getting very, very autumnal in profile, and the tannins seeming to have completely melted away. They really went through an eight to ten year stretch where they seemed to have reached the far side of the plateau and likely to need drinking up over the short term. Then around 2010 one saw another stage develop in the wines, with the sweetness and depth of fruit really returning, the structures perking up again (after a very lethargic structural decade) and the wines showing far more mid-palate stuffing and grip than they had during their hiatus. Red Burgundy, IME, never ages in a linear progression and the 1985s just went into a phase that looked like they were nearing the end, only to bounce back with gusto! A total surprise to me, given how they looked to be fading big time around age twenty, and I remember chatting with Veronique Drouhin four or five years ago and she mentioning how excited she was to also see the 1985s starting to show the depth and grip that they had seemingly let go of during their vintage vacation. Today, the vintage is back at the top of its game and along with 1978, probably the nicest mature vintage for drinking at its absolute apogee.

All the Best,

John

posts.name: Stuart BeauneHead Niemtzow
posts.cooked:
John Gilman:

The '85 Burgs were rightly praised out of the blocks and so many were drunk young, not because of their short projected windows of drinkability in the WA, but because they were so flat out delicious in their youth, with such stunningly pure and seductive fruit tones that it was almost impossible to resist opening them in that first five to ten year span. at the top of its game and along with 1978, probably the nicest mature vintage for drinking at its absolute apogee.

John, I think that grossly oversimplifies the situation at the time the '85s came out. Were that the criterion…“flat out delicious”…many ageworthy vintages would never get the age they are worthy of: they’d all be gobbled up.

The Burgundy consumer at the time was essentially dependent on Parker and to a much lesser degree, Coates, at the time for guidance on vintages. Parker unveiled vintages…and provided very specific notes, with ridiculous drinking windows. (It was a like a landrush in Burgundy in early 1988…thanks to Parker’s praise of the vintage, and there was little to be had there.) I know because I followed many of them, as did almost everyone I knew. (By the time the '88s came out, after visiting and talking to producers, I realized just how “off” Parker was on his projections…and his evaluations of the 1983s, and gave almost all vintages a minimum of 15 years…and above.)

Parker lauded the 1985s in Burgundy much as he had lauded the '82s in Bordeaux. (The similarities are apt: in Burgundy the new winemaking generation was just taking over, and with their input and that of the weather, it was the first fruity vintage in ages there; new hygeine practices helped, too; those that were still struggling with their hygeine made wines that were hardly “impossible to resist” in '85; I got a bunch of them, too…and wish, now I had kept them.).

To ascribe such independent thinking to Burgundy consumers in 1988 and beyond with the '85’s…I think…misportrays the situation in the wine world at the time, though. Everyone was looking for insights…and Parker was it. In retrospect, he was advocating drinking wines way way too young, IMO…and that’s not just a matter of taste.

I think people learned their lessons by the time 1990 was released…and…realized that beyond pure present pleasure…there was great potential behind all the fruit and seductive charms of young red Burgundy wines.

FWIW

posts.name: harry spithoven
posts.cooked: Lafite 85, stil very young…

posts.name: Jerry Hey
posts.cooked: I had the 85 Pierre Morey Montrachet and the 85 Henri Jayer Echezeaux last weekend. Both were still in great shape; the Montrachet was in pristine condition with a relatively light color for a 30 year old wine but it never seemed to really open up to the highest of heights I was hoping for, The 85 Echezeaux was brought back directly from Jayer’s cellar and the bottle looked like a brand new release; the wine had beautiful ruby color and the purity of fruit was showing well. Two 85’s that are still in great shape.

posts.name: Jon Lawrence
posts.cooked: A very good friend of mine who has been drinking great wine almost as long as I have been alive recently told me the 10’s will be very similar to the 85’s, great young drinkers, and will probably not shut down and just keep getting better.

posts.name: John Gilman
posts.cooked:
Jon Lawrence:

A very good friend of mine who has been drinking great wine almost as long as I have been alive recently told me the 10’s will be very similar to the 85’s, great young drinkers, and will probably not shut down and just keep getting better.

Hi Jon,

The 2010s IME are structured quite a bit differently than the 1985s- more concentration of fruit (though equally pure to '85) and better acids, so more tightly-knit and intensely flavored than the previous vintage. I would peg them significantly higher in ultimate quality than the 1985s, but also many have shut down now (as the '85s did not do in their early days) and I have no plans on opening any of mine until they are at least twenty years of age. As Stuart points out, Burgundy really needs longer than is customarily thought to really hit its plateau of peak maturity, and it would be a shame to waste the 2010s too early on and only enjoy a small percentage of the potential that they have. If your friend had said that they will be every bit as great as the best '85s, I would certainly agree with him, but I think the wines are quite different in style. If you want a vintage with much of '85s early succulence and gorgeous fruit tones, 2014 would be a more appropriate comparison I think.

All the Best,

John
